title: Orange and almond cake, flourless
subtitle: A dense, moist cake built on whole boiled oranges and ground almonds
excerpt: A 23 cm round cake made by boiling two oranges whole until completely tender, blending them skin and all, and folding into a batter of ground almonds, eggs, and sugar. No flour, no butter. The peel's bitterness and the almond's richness balance the sugar. Naturally gluten-free.
sourceNotes: Flourless citrus and almond cakes are documented across Sephardic Jewish and Moorish-influenced Spanish and North African baking, where avoiding wheat and dairy together has been a centuries-old cooking tradition. The whole-boiled-orange technique (cooking the fruit completely before blending) is a Spanish and Moroccan adaptation that removes the bitterness from the peel through extended simmering. No single British public-domain source covers this specific method. The recipe here draws on the public-domain Sephardic baking tradition as documented in pre-1928 historical culinary sources from Spain and North Africa, adapted to a standard 23 cm tin format.

recipe.freezeNotes: Keeps very well frozen; the high fat and moisture content from the almonds and whole orange survives freezing without texture change.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The boiled oranges can be prepared up to 3 days ahead and refrigerated whole. The baked cake keeps for 4 days at room temperature.

recipeTools.notes: 23 cm round tin, greased and lined.
Or a food processor — the orange pulp needs to be fully smooth.

glossaryTerms.definition: Cooking an orange, skin on, in simmering water for 1.5–2 hours until the skin is completely tender and the fruit is soft throughout. The long cooking removes the harsh bitterness from the pith and peel. The whole fruit : flesh, juice, and skin (seeds removed) is blended into a purée for baking.

content.text: This cake begins two hours before you make the batter, by simmering two whole oranges until the skin is completely tender. A

is blended skin and all — the essential technique that makes this cake different from a cake made with zest and juice. The peel contributes flavour, body, and a faint bitterness that prevents the ground almonds from being cloying. The result is a very moist, dense cake with a flavour that is orange through and through.

There is no flour and no butter. The ground almonds provide the structure; the eggs provide the lift. The cake is naturally gluten-free, and it keeps for four days at room temperature without drying out.

items.prepNote: large, unwaxed — boil whole, then blend
1 teaspoon — use a gluten-free variety if strictly required

content.text: Place the two {{orange}} whole unwaxed oranges in a medium saucepan and cover completely with cold water. Bring to a boil, reduce to a steady simmer, and cook for 1 hour 30 minutes, adding more water as needed to keep the oranges submerged. They are ready when a skewer passes through the skin with no resistance at all and the orange feels completely soft. Remove and leave to cool. When cool, cut in half, remove any seeds, and blend : skin, flesh, and all the juice to a completely smooth purée with a stick blender or food processor.

Heat the oven to 175°C fan. Grease and line a 23 cm round tin. Beat the {{eggs}} eggs and {{caster-sugar}} caster sugar together until well combined and slightly lighter in colour — about 2 minutes with a hand mixer or whisk. Stir in the orange purée. Fold in the {{almond-flour}} ground almonds, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. The batter will be quite runny for a cake; this is correct.

Pour the batter into the prepared tin. Bake for 50–55 minutes, until the top is a deep golden-brown, the edges have pulled away from the tin slightly, and a skewer through the centre comes out clean. The cake will be very moist inside; a clean skewer is the cue. Cool in the tin for 30 minutes before turning out; the lack of gluten makes it fragile when hot. Dust with icing sugar before serving.
